result.markdown:
---
title: "Use neuroim data with NumPy / SciPy / scikit-learn / matplotlib"
subtitle: "Cross into the array world once, on purpose, with the frame-faithful accessor."
jupyter: python3
sidebar: cookbook
---

## Goal

You hold a typed `neuroim` container and you want to run a NumPy,
SciPy, scikit-learn, or matplotlib operation on the numbers. The
scientific stack speaks `numpy.ndarray`. Cross that boundary in one
explicit step and know exactly which array shape you handed over.

## Why the explicit step exists

`np.asarray(vol)` does **not** return the voxel data. A typed image
refuses implicit array conversion — because the alternative is
silently dropping the `NeuroSpace`, which is the failure the typed
contract exists to prevent.

::: {#779f3db3 .cell execution_count=1}
``` {.python .cell-code}
from pathlib import Path
import sys

sys.path.insert(0, str(Path.cwd().parents[1] / "src"))

import numpy as np
import neuroim as ni

repo = Path.cwd().parents[1] if Path.cwd().name == "cookbook" else Path.cwd()
bold = ni.read_image(str(repo / "golden_tests/fixtures/tiny_bold.nii.gz"))

try:
    np.asarray(bold)
except TypeError as exc:
    print(exc)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}
```
DenseNeuroVec does not implicitly convert to a NumPy array: np.asarray()/np.array() would silently drop the spatial frame (NeuroSpace). Use .data to get the underlying ndarray explicitly.
```
:::
:::


The refusal names the accessor to call. That message is the contract,
not an error to work around.

## The frame-faithful accessors

Each container has one accessor that returns the ndarray at its
**natural rank** — the array `np.asarray` would have produced if
implicit conversion were allowed:

| Container | Accessor | Returned shape |
|---|---|---|
| `NeuroVol` (3-D) | `.as_array()` | `(x, y, z)` |
| `NeuroVec` (4-D) | `.data` | `(x, y, z, t)` |
| `NeuroHyperVec` (5-D+) | `.data` | `(x, y, z, t, …)` |

::: {#7e87c2b1 .cell execution_count=2}
``` {.python .cell-code}
mask = ni.read_image(str(repo / "golden_tests/fixtures/tiny_mask.nii.gz"), type="vol")

vol_arr = mask.as_array()        # 3-D ndarray
bold_arr = bold.data             # 4-D ndarray, frame-faithful

(vol_arr.shape, bold_arr.shape)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=2}
```
((8, 8, 4), (8, 8, 4, 10))
```
:::
:::


## SciPy / scikit-learn

Hand the ndarray to the library directly. `.data` is the 4-D array;
when an estimator wants a 2-D `(samples, features)` matrix, ask for
that shape explicitly with `.as_matrix()` so the reshape is in *your*
code, not hidden:

::: {#5a197884 .cell execution_count=3}
``` {.python .cell-code}
from scipy import ndimage

# SciPy on the 3-D mask: label connected components.
labels, n = ndimage.label(mask.as_array())

# scikit-learn-style 2-D matrix: voxels x timepoints, asked for by name.
mat = bold.as_matrix()
(n, mat.shape)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=3}
```
(1, (256, 10))
```
:::
:::


`.data` and `.as_matrix()` are different on purpose: `.data` is the
frame-faithful array; `.as_matrix()` is the deliberate
voxels-by-timepoints matricization for 2-D estimators. Pick the one
whose shape you want; neither is reached by accident.

## matplotlib

::: {#a3f2c1ea .cell execution_count=4}
``` {.python .cell-code}
import matplotlib
matplotlib.use("Agg")
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

mid = bold.data[:, :, bold.shape[2] // 2, 0]
fig, ax = plt.subplots()
ax.imshow(mid.T, origin="lower")
ax.set_title("axial slice, t=0")
fig.savefig("_slice.png", dpi=110)
print("wrote _slice.png", mid.shape)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}
```
wrote _slice.png (8, 8)
```
:::
:::


## What's guaranteed

- The accessor returns a real `numpy.ndarray`; from there the whole
  scientific stack works normally.
- The crossing is in your code, at a named call. Nothing downstream
  silently dropped the spatial frame to get there.
- Going back: rebuild a typed container from a result array with
  `ni.DenseNeuroVol(result, vol.space)` /
  `ni.DenseNeuroVec(result, vec.space)` to re-attach the frame and
  resume the typed contract.

## Trapdoors

- A library that calls `np.asarray()` on the container *internally*
  raises the same refusal from inside its own stack trace. Convert
  first, then pass the ndarray — do not pass the typed object into
  array-only code.
- `.as_matrix()` on a `NeuroVec` returns `(n_voxels, n_time)`, not the
  4-D array. If you wanted the volume-shaped data, use `.data`.
- A result ndarray has no `NeuroSpace`. It is just numbers until you
  wrap it back in a typed container with the originating `.space`.

## See also

- [Concepts: Typed images](../concepts/images.qmd) — why implicit array conversion is refused.
- [Interop with nibabel](interop-with-nibabel.qmd) — the other boundary, in both directions.
- [Save a derived map with provenance](save-derived-map-with-provenance.qmd) — re-attaching the frame and keeping a Receipt.
